CHAPTER 47
COMMONWEALTH STUDENTS IN BRITAIN 1963-64

S TUDENTS from other parts of the Commonwealth comprise nearly two-thirds of all overseas students in Britain. An overseas student is defined as a person over the age of 18 years whose permanent home address is overseas and whose purpose in coming to Britain is to undertake full-time study, research or training for a minimum period of six months.
Table 1 gives details of Commonwealth students by country of origin and category of study.
Table 2 gives a more detailed analysis of Commonwealth students at British Universities.

These Tables have been compiled from figures provided, in respect of the Universities, by the Association of Commonwealth Universities, and, in respect of other Institutions, by the British Council and the London Conference on Overseas Students; and it is with their permission that the information contained herein is reproduced.

Many overseas students enroll privately with the Inns of Court, with Private Colleges and Institutions, and for Nursing, Professional and Practical Training. Some of these may therefore not come to the notice of the London Conference on Overseas Students; and for this reason the figures for students under these headings are approximate only.
Further information about overseas students may be obtained from the Commonwealth Universities Yearbook , 1965, and from Overseas Students in Britain , 1963–64.

CHAPTER 48
COMMONWEALTH WEIGHTS AND MEASURES

IMPERIAL and Metric units of measurement of length, area, volume, capacity, mass or weight and measurements of electricity for use in Britain are laid down in Schedules to the Weights and Measures Act 1963.

Similar measures are in use in Canada, Australia, New Zealand, Ceylon, Ghana, Nigeria, Sierra Leone, Jamaica, Trinidad and Tobago, Tanganyika and Zanzibar, Uganda, Kenya, Malawi, Zambia and Southern Rhodesia although in some cases units of measurement no longer in use in Britain have been retained. The hundredweight, in Canada, weighs 100 lbs whilst in Southern Rhodesia the Cental of 100 lbs is used instead of the hundredweight. In Southern Rhodesia too, the Cord (128 lbs) is in common use, as it is also in Canada. In Quebec the following French measurements are in use: 1 French (or Paris) Foot = 12.789 inches; 180 French Feet = 1 Arpent.

India. India has adopted the metric system of weights and measures in replacement for the standards contained in the Indian Standards and Weights Act of 1939. Under the 1939 Act the official standard unit of weight was the tola of 180 grains (7,000 grains = 1 lb), and from it were derived the heavier weights e.g.

5 tolas 1 chhatak
16 chhataks 1 seer (2.2 lbs)
40 seers 1 maund
Other local standards were however in common use throughout India, e.g. the Bazar maund of 82 2/7 lbs, whilst the Factory maund weighed 74 2/3 lbs.
For linear measurement the Imperial yard was the standard unit throughout India. In Bengal 1 gandah = 2 1 /4 sq. ft.; 1 chhatak = 5 sq. yds.; 1 katha = 40 sq. yds. and 1 bigha = 1,600 sq. yds.
Metric conversion tables for the various units in use were published by the Indian Ministry of Commerce and Industry in August 1962.
The monetary units shown below are the same, and are expressed in the same way, in both India and Pakistan:

Re. 1 1s. 6d.
Rs. 100 £7 10s.
Rs. 1,000 £75
Rs. 1,00,000 (one lakh) £7,500
Rs. 10,00,000 (ten lakhs) £75,000
Rs. 1,00,00,000 (one crore) £750,000
Pakistan. Pakistan continues to use the official measures contained in the Indian Standards and Weights Act of 1939, based on the standard tola of 180 grains.
Ceylon. Imperial measures are in general use in Ceylon but local Sinhalese and Tamil systems of measurement are used in certain areas.

1943 to August 1945; Captain; Higher Executive Officer ( in absentia ) 22nd July 1940; Private Secretary to Parliamentary Under-Secretary of State December 1945 to December 1946; Principal, Burma Office, 2nd December 1946; Rangoon, Burma, October 1947 to January 1948; seconded to Foreign Office (British Embassy, Rangoon) January to April 1948; Canberra May 1952 to July 1955; Calcutta (local Assistant Secretary) 9th January 1958 to August 1961; Assistant Secretary 1st April 1960; Inspector 13th November 1961.

WICKSON, Dalby Walter Harry, MBE (1951); born 3rd July 1906; educated Bedford Modern School 1918–24; m. 1930 Irene Walduck (3 s. 1932, 1934, 1945; 1 d. 1937); S.C. and T., G.P.O., July 1924; Clerical Officer, Colonial Office, 12th May 1930; Higher Clerical Officer 6th July 1940; served in R.A.S.C. 1942–46; Sergeant; attached to War Office for duty with Malayan Planning Staff August 1944; released March 1946; Staff Officer (later Higher Executive Officer), Dominions Office, 19th February 1947; Passport Officer, Cape Town, December 1949 to May 1953; Senior Executive Officer 18th May 1953; Delhi March 1958 to March 1962; Chief Executive Officer (local) 1st July 1961; Chief Executive Officer (temporary) 30th July 1962, (acting) 11th October 1963.

WIDDOWS, Leslie Spencer; born 16th November 1912; educated St. Bartholomew's Grammar School, Newbury, 1924–29; m. 1937 Nanette Thacker (1 d. 1938; 2 s. 1945, 1947); Social Welfare appointments with Southend C. B. Council and Herts and Berks County Councils 1929–37; Certificate, Poor Law Examinations Board, 1936; Social Welfare Officer, Portsmouth City Council, 1937 and Warwickshire County Council, 1939–48; commissioned as Navigator 1943–45; Executive Officer, National Assistance Board, July 1948; Higher Executive Officer, Colonial Office, 25th November 1957; Commonwealth Relations Office 1st December 1960; Calcutta March 1961 to May 1964.

WILLCOCKS, Derek; born 14th June 1930; educated Plymouth College and Devonport High School; m. 1956 Sylvia Freeman (1 s. 1957; 2 d. 1959, 1961); National Service with Royal Air Force 1949–51; Executive Officer, Ministry of Supply, June 1951 (seniority from 1st April 1949); Ministry of Aviation October 1959; Higher Executive Officer, Commonwealth Relations Office, 17th October 1960; Karachi February 1962.
WILLCOCKS, Lieutenant-Colonel John Francis, OBE (1962); born 26th August 1918; educated Radley College and R.M.A., Woolwich; m. 1940 Betty Lloyd (1 s. 1942; 1 d. 1944); commissioned Royal

Artillery 1938; served N.W. Europe and Middle East 1939–47; qualified Staff College 1948; served Singapore 1949–52; Directing Staff, Staff College, 1954–57; qualified Armed Forces Staff College, U.S.A., 1957; served B.A.O.R. 1957–59; Commanding Officer, 26th Regiment, R.A., Malaya, 1959–62; Assistant to Principal Staff Officer to Secretary of State for Commonwealth Relations 1st October 1962.

WILLIAMS, John Robert; born 15th September 1922; educated Sheen County School and Fitzwilliam House, Cambridge; Geographical Tripos Part I Class II Division I; Archaeological and Anthropological Tripos Part II Class I; m. 1958 Helga Elizabeth Konow Lund (2 d. 1959, 1962; 1 s. 1960); military service 1941–46; enlisted in ranks of King's Royal Rifle Corps; commissioned service with 1st Battalion King's African Rifles in East and Central Africa; Ceylon and Burma campaign; Captain; Assistant Principal, Colonial Office, August 1949; Secretary, Singapore Riots Inquiry Commission, 1951; Principal 26th November 1951; visited Somaliland, Nyasaland and Southern Rhodesia 1954; visited Addis Ababa for negotiation of Anglo/Ethiopian Agreement on Somali Grazing Areas 1954; seconded to Commonwealth Relations Office as Principal 2nd March 1956; Delhi March 1956; transferred to Commonwealth Relations Office 24th June 1958; Deputy High Commissioner, Penang, August 1959 to October 1962; Kuala Lumpur October 1962; Delhi (local Assistant Secretary) May 1963; Assistant Secretary 2nd April 1964.

WILLIAMS, Martin John; born 3rd November 1941; educated Manchester Grammar School 1952–59 and Corpus Christi College, Oxford, 1959–63; Classical Moderations Class II, Lit. Hum. Class II; m. 1964; Assistant Principal, Commonwealth Relations Office, 19th August 1963.

WILLIAMS, Reginald Dennis; born 23rd December 1931; educated Helston Grammar School 1943–48; m. 1958 Irene Elizabeth Steen (1 d. 1961; 1 s. 1963); Temporary Postal and Telegraph Officer, General Post Office, 1948–49; Clerical Officer, Admiralty, 1949–53; National Service 1950–52; Executive Officer, Customs and Excise, 1953–61; on loan to Ministry of Power November 1956 to May 1957; Executive Officer, Commonwealth Relations Office, 1st November 1961; Accra (local Higher Executive Officer) May 1963; Higher Executive Officer 1st September 1963.

WILLIAMS, Trevor Ernest Frank; born 6th December 1928; educated Wilson's Grammar School, Horsham and London; Royal Air Force 1947–49; served in Malta and North Africa; Clerical Officer, Control Office for Germany and Austria,
Digitized by Google
CHAPTER 50
Biographical Notes

Foreign Office (German Section), September 1946 to March 1952; Crown Agents for Oversea Governments and Administrations April 1952 to April 1962; Executive Officer December 1956; Commonwealth Relations Office 24th April 1962; Karachi May 1962 to May 1964; Higher Executive Officer Notional 14th August 1964.

WINDLE, Anthony Peter William; born 22nd February 1928; m. 1951 Helen Laing (2 s. 1952, 1958); Sorting Clerk and Telegraphist, General Post Office, 2nd January 1945; National Service with Royal Air Force (Signals) March 1946 to August 1948; Postal and Telegraph Officer 1949; Clerical Officer, Commonwealth Relations Office, 15th August 1949; Colombo May 1951 to July 1954; Karachi July 1956 to July 1958; Bombay (local Executive Officer) July 1959 to October 1961; Executive Officer September 1959; Kingston June 1962; Higher Executive Officer 14th August 1964.

WINTER, Arnold Martin; born 22nd December 1920; educated Upton House Central School, London; m. 1951 Rita Hillringhaus (1 s. 1951; 1 d. 1956); war service in R.A.F. 1941–46; Temporary Clerk II, Control Commission for Germany, September 1946; Control Officer III 10th May 1948; Clerical Officer, Commonwealth Relations Office, 14th June 1951; Karachi October 1953 to March 1958; Executive Officer 24th December 1954; Colombo May 1959 to June 1961; Higher Executive Officer 29th January 1962; Nicosia September 1963.

WOLSTENHOLME, Alan; born 3rd November 1923; educated Kimberworth Senior Boys' School, South Yorkshire; m. 1960 Rita Kathleen Grimshaw (1 d. 1961); Junior Reporter, Rotherham Express , 1939; wartime service, Merchant Navy, 1942–46; Reporter, Yorkshire Evening News , 1946–48; Reporter, Straits Times , Singapore, 1948–51; Sub-Editor, Reuters, London, 1951–52; Chief Reporter and subsequently News Editor, Malay Mail , Kuala Lumpur, 1953–63; Temporary Information Officer, Commonwealth Relations Office, 28th May 1963; Kuala Lumpur May 1963; Information Officer 30th December 1963; Kuching January 1964; Kuala Lumpur January 1964.

WOOL-LEWIS, Cyril Eric, OBE (1951); born 17th June 1906; educated Westminster School and Trinity College, Cambridge, 1925–29; commissioned in East Surrey Regiment 1929; Assistant Commissioner of Police, Sierra Leone, 1935; Assistant District Commissioner, Sierra Leone, 1937; District Commissioner 1942; Commissioner of the Turks and Caicos Islands, B.W.I., 1946–51; seconded to the Colonial Office 1951–53; Administrative Officer Class II, Northern Nigeria, 1953; Administrative Officer Class I 1954; trans-

ferred to Nigerian Federal Government Service 1956; Staff Grade 1956; Permanent Secretary 1957; retired from Overseas Civil Service June 1961; Temporary Principal, Commonwealth Relations Office, 23rd October 1961.

WOOLVERTON, Kenneth Arthur Frederick; born 4th August 1926; educated Orange Hill Grammar School 1938–44; m. Kathleen Eleanor West; Meteorologist, R.A.F., 1944–48; Hendon Borough Council 1948–50; Clerical Officer, Colonial Office, 3rd April 1950; Executive Officer 19th February 1952; Higher Executive Officer 11th July 1960; Commonwealth Relations Office 18th December 1961; Kingston May 1964.

WRIGHT, John Arthur, born 10th January 1932; educated Heanor Grammar School, Derbyshire; m. 1956 Enfys Ann Roberts (1s. 1962); Clerical Officer, Colonial Office, May 1948; National Service (Royal Air Force) 1950–52; Librarian IV, Commonwealth Relations Office, September 1959; Librarian III September 1962.

WYTHERS, John Rowland Graham, BEM (1945); born 2nd February 1918; educated Soham Grammar School, Cambs., 1929–34; m. 1945 Joyce Warren (1 s. 1946; 1 d. 1948); L.C.C. Social Welfare Department 1934–39; Relieving Officers' Certificate, Poor Law Examinations Board, 1936; Territorial Army (Royal Artillery, Heavy A.A.) 1939–42; served in Norway and North Africa; Intelligence Corps 1942; served in Persia and Iraq (Warrant Officer); L.C.C. Social Welfare Department 1946–47; Assistant Area Meals Officer, L.C.C. Civic Restaurants, 1st May 1947; Executive Officer, National Assistance Board, 12th July 1948; Higher Executive Officer, Commonwealth Relations Office, 15th December 1958; Chief Registrar 1st January 1959; Bombay December 1959 to March 1963; Senior Executive Officer (acting) 16th September 1963.
Y

YOUNG, Eric; born 16th November 1924; educated Maltby Grammar School, Yorkshire, 1935–42 and Sheffield University 1942–43 and 1946–48; Honours Class II Division I in English Language and Literature 1948; BA; m. 1949 Sheila Hutchinson (1 d. 1956; 2 s. 1958, 1961); Royal Navy 1943–46; served in Mediterranean, Indian Ocean and Far East; joined Sheffield Telegraph as reporter and leader-writer July 1948; sub-editor, Western Morning News , Plymouth, 1951; sub-editor and later regional news editor, Guardian , Manchester, 1953–58; visited West Africa as reporter 1958; Public Relations Officer, National Coal Board, Manchester, 1958–59; Temporary Senior Information Officer, Colonial Office, and Deputy Director, United Kingdom Information Office in
Digitized by Google

Tanganyika and Zanzibar, January 1960; Senior Information Officer March 1961; Commonwealth Relations Office as Deputy Director, British Information Services, Tanganyika, December 1961 to September 1963; Regional Information Officer (local Principal Information Officer), Kaduna, November 1963.

YOUNG, Joffre Alfred; born 26th March 1915; educated Merton School; m. 1941 Ethel Violet Welfare; Post Office 1929; Clerical Officer, Savings Bank Department, 1938; Clerical Officer, Canterbury Telephone Area, 1948; Higher Clerical Officer, Shrewsbury Telephone Area, 1950; Higher Executive Officer, (Treasury Pooling
Scheme), Commonwealth Relations Office, 11th September 1961; Dacca October 1962.

YOUNG, William Dempster; born 13th October 1912; m. 1940 Patricia Harrison (2 d. 1941, 1947; 1 s. 1945); enlisted Royal Artillery 1933, retired with rank of Major 1948; Clerical Officer 19th December 1947; Delhi January 1948 to August 1950; Karachi September 1951 to July 1955; Executive Officer 3rd September 1955; Delhi November 1955 to November 1957; Kuala Lumpur April 1958 to August 1961; Karachi April 1962; Higher Executive Officer (notional) 1st September 1963; Higher Executive Officer 25th November 1963.

ON THE BEAM

Laser rangefinding was one of the first uses suggested when the laser principle was discovered: rangefinders will be among the earliest operational applications. Advantages include independence of good light conditions, high accuracy at longer ranges and the ability to operate undetected.

Barr & Stroud invented the modern optical rangefinder and are themselves in the forefront of British laser development. Now they offer laser rangefinders, having come to an agreement with the Hughes Aircraft Company of California, who discovered the laser principle.
